full_response:
Step-by-step solution

1. Choice of coordinates in the inertial (“laboratory”) frame  
   We work in the usual cylindrical spatial coordinates  
   (t , r , θ , z), where t is the coordinate time measured by static laboratory clocks and the z–axis coincides with the axis of the merry-go-round.  

2. Radius of the rim  
   Let R be the (constant) radius of the edge of the merry-go-round as measured in the laboratory frame, so every point on the rim satisfies r = R at all times.  
   For the motion to be physically admissible relativistically we must have the tangential speed
      v = ω R < c .                                 (1)

3. Direction of rotation  
   The problem statement says the platform “is spinning clockwise.”  Looking down the +z-axis a clockwise rotation corresponds to decreasing θ, i.e. θ̇ = –ω.  (If one prefers the opposite convention, simply change the sign of ω.)

4. World-line in cylindrical coordinates  
   Pick one particular rider who at t = 0 is located at azimuthal angle θ0 on the rim (z = 0 plane).  Then

      t(t) = t ,                                              (2a)
      r(t) = R ,                                             (2b)
      θ(t) = θ0 – ω t ,                                      (2c)
      z(t) = 0 .                                             (2d)

   Eq. (2) is the wanted parametric representation with the inertial time t used as the parameter.

5. Optional expression in Cartesian coordinates  
   Transforming (r , θ) → (x , y) gives

      x(t) = R cos(θ0 – ω t) ,                               (3a)
      y(t) = R sin(θ0 – ω t) ,                               (3b)
      z(t) = 0 ,                                             (3c)
      t(t) = t .                                             (3d)

   In spacetime, this trajectory is a helix whose pitch is c Δt while its spatial projection is the circle of radius R traced at constant angular speed ω.

6. Note on proper time of the rider (not asked but useful)  
   The rider’s proper time τ is related to t by
      dτ = √{1 – (ω R / c)^2} dt ,
   reflecting the time dilation associated with the tangential speed v = ω R.

\boxed{t=t,\;\; r(t)=R,\;\; \theta(t)=\theta_{0}-\omega t,\;\; z(t)=0\qquad (\text{with }v=\omega R<c)}
